name: Sanabrese

ancestors.notes: Medieval Leonese: the direct genetic ancestor, brought to the Portucale region during the Reconquista resettlement. This variety is the trunk from which Mirandese, Rionorese, and Guadramilese descend.
Galaico-Portuguese adstrate: contributed nasal vowels, lh/nh digraph convention, and diphthong contractions.
Old Portuguese adstrate: Portuguese orthographic conventions (lh, nh, ã) penetrated the writing tradition.
Gallaecian Celtic substrate of NW Iberia, shared through the Leonese parent.
Mozarabic: Romance spoken by Christians under Moorish rule in the Leonese-reclaimed Douro corridor.

notes: Sanabrese (Sanabrés), Asturleonese occidental spoken in the Sanabria comarca (Zamora, Spain) and Portuguese-side sub-zones (Guadramil, Rio de Onor). Classified as hablas de Sanabria within Asturleonese occidental (Frías-Conde, El Habla de Sanabria, p. 2). All claims below are stated-explicitly in Frías-Conde unless noted. KEY PHONOLOGICAL FEATURES grounded in the Krüger-era Sanabrese description as analysed by Frías-Conde: (1) F- RETENTION: Latin initial /f-/ fully retained — *fillo, figo, forno, feleito, fuogo, facer, falar* (p. 4). Contrast with Castilian /h-/∅; Sanabrese pattern matches Galician-Portuguese. CONFIDENCE: HIGH. (2) INITIAL L- > /ʎ/ (⟨ll-⟩): *llama, llameiro, llariego, lleitón, lluogo, llobo, lluz* (p. 4). ALL initial Latin L- palatalized. CONFIDENCE: HIGH. (3) INITIAL N- > /ɲ/ (⟨ñ-⟩): *ñabo, ñiebla, ñiego, ñube, ñegro, ñuoite* (p. 4). Initial nasal palatalization is productive. CONFIDENCE: HIGH. (4) PL-/KL-/FL- > /tʃ/ (⟨ch-⟩): *chamar, chave, chover, chuvia, cheirar, chorar, cheno* (p. 4). CONFIDENCE: HIGH. (5) FOUR-WAY SIBILANT SYSTEM: /s/, /ʃ/, /θ/, /tʃ/ — all four present and distinct (inferred from consonant inventory, p. 3). Sanabrese has /s/~/ʃ/ distinction (unlike Castilian which lacks /ʃ/) AND /θ/ (unlike Galician-Portuguese which lacks /θ/). CONFIDENCE: inferred-from-inventory. (6) TONIC DIPHTHONGS: Latin Ĕ → /je/: *piedra, tierra* (p. 3). Latin Ŏ → /wo/ (NOT /we/): *cuorpo, ruoda, puode, lluogo* — the /wo/ form is MORE ARCHAIC than Castilian /we/ (p. 3). CONFIDENCE: HIGH. (7) FINAL VOWEL SYSTEM — THREE-WAY: final vowels reduced to /e/, /a/, /U/ (reduced /u/) — not the five-way system of stressed syllables (p. 2–3). Atonic /u/ → /U/ (centralised/reduced). CONFIDENCE: HIGH. (8) /kt/ > /it/ GENERALLY: *feito, peito, lleite* (p. 4). EXCEPTION/SUB-ZONE: Vigo de Sanabria has /kt/ > /oit/: *moito, escoita, troita, coida* (p. 4) — a conservative sub-zone feature. CONFIDENCE: HIGH for both. (9) K'L-/G'L-/LJ-/-LL- > /ʎ/: *muller, coller, abella, traballo, palla* — Sanabrese has /ʎ/ for ALL three positions (L-, -LL-, K'L-), unlike asturiano occidental which has /ts/ for L- and -LL- (comparative table, p. 4). CONFIDENCE: HIGH. NOTE: -IELLO suffix preserved (not reduced to -illo as in Castilian): *castiello, palombiella* (p. 3). -AVI-/-AI-/-ARJ- > -eiro: *diñeiro, moliñeiro* (p. 3). -n- > -ñ- in -INU suffix: *camiñeiro, moliñeiro, fariñudo* (p. 4). SUB-ZONE VARIATION: Guadramil (PT side) shows more extreme /wo/ diphthong: *cuorpo, cuarda, muarto* (p. 3). Vigo de Sanabria: /kt/ > /oit/ (moito). Rionor (-ía > -í): *sabí, falarí* vs. general *teníe, veníe* (p. 3).

sources.author: Frías-Conde, Xavier
sources.title: El Habla de Sanabria
sources.publisher: CES Don Bosco (UCM); ISR Romania Minor (conference/seminar handout, 7 pp.)
sources.notes: Primary source for all Sanabrese phonological claims. Carries the Krüger-era Sanabrese phonological description as systematised by Frías-Conde. All feature attributions in notes above are page-referenced. Year is approximate (undated document).

sources.author: García Arias, X.Ll.
sources.title: Gramática histórica de la lengua asturiana
